elements.sent-2411-001: The aim of this work is to provide the contributors to journals or to multi-authored books with an easy-to-use and flexible class file compatible with \LaTeX\ and \AmS-\LaTeX.
elements.sent-2411-002: This paper analyzes a two-by-two Temple-type system of conservation laws with discontinuous flux, focusing on applications in traffic modeling.
elements.sent-2411-003: We prove the existence of entropy solutions for initial data with sufficiently small total variation.
elements.sent-2411-004: Additionally, we explicitly construct the corresponding Riemann solver and examine its key properties.
elements.sent-2411-005: To illustrate the impact of the discontinuous flux on the solution, we also present numerical simulations.
elements.sent-2411-006: Conservation laws with discontinuous flux functions arise in various real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-007: These applications span multiple fields, including traffic flow, multi-phase fluid dynamics (e.g., two-phase flow models and environmental engineering), material science (e.g., crystallization processes and composite materials), and systems engineering (e.g., acoustic waves and electromagnetic waves).
elements.sent-2411-008: In particular, traffic systems often exhibit sudden changes in road conditions, speed limits, or traffic regulations, which can be effectively modelled using conservation laws with discontinuous flux.
elements.sent-2411-009: In this paper we study the one-dimensional $2\times2$ system of conservation laws with discontinuous flux \begin{equation}\begin{cases} \partial_t\rho + \partial_x \left( c \, V(h) \, \rho \right)=0, \partial_t\left( \rho \left( h + p(\rho)\right)\right) + \partial_x \left( c \, V(h) \, \rho \left( h + p(\rho)\right) \right)=0. \end{cases} \label{e:model}\end{equation} This system finds its relevance in the modeling of vehicular traffic.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation} \partial_t\rho + \partial_x \left( c \, V(h) \, \rho \right)=0, \partial_t\left( \rho \left( h + p(\rho)\right)\right) + \partial_x \left( c \, V(h) \, \rho \left( h + p(\rho)\right) \right)=0.  \label{e:model}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-010: Indeed, it can be interpreted as a generalisation of two second order vehicular traffic models: the one proposed by Aw, Rascle \cite{AwRascle2000 and Zhang \cite{zhang2002TRB , and the one kinetically derived by Chiarello, G\"ottlich, Schillinger and Tosin in \cite{ChiarelloCMS23 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: We refer to the former as the ARZ model, and to the latter as the CGST model.
elements.sent-2411-012: In this context, $t \geq 0$ denotes the time, $x \in \R$ the space, $\rho=\rho(t,x) \geq 0$ the density, $c=c(x) >0$ is a given discontinuous function that mimics the changes in the road conditions, $p=p(\rho) \geq 0$ is the \lq\lq pressure\rq\rq\ function, and $V \geq 0$ represents the speed law.
elements.sent-2411-013: The interpretation of $h=h(t,x) \geq 0$ depends on the specific model under consideration: it represents the velocity in the ARZ model and the mean headway in the CGST model, see Section~\ref{s:appl for further details.}
